text:
LHAnimationClipPlayer is a feature for playing AnimationClips independently.

It has a simple and minimal configuration, consisting only of AnimationClip and Animator, requiring no unnecessary assets or components.
Furthermore, because it can directly reflect the start and end states before Awake,
it is ideal for UI in/out animations.

Dialogs aren't a separate feature; they're simply one way of displaying content using the ScreenStack functionality.\n\nIf you need to implement complex dependencies, you can define your own LifetimeScope for this dialog.

Overlays aren't a separate feature; they're simply one way of expressing things using the ScreenStack functionality.\n\nIf you need to implement complex dependencies, you can define your own LifetimeScope for this overlay.

Pop-ups aren't a separate feature; they're a way to display content using the ScreenStack functionality.\nThey're useful for focusing on a specific location on the base screen or displaying small windows.

If you use TransitionScene while a Dialog is open, the Dialog will automatically resume when you BackScene into that scene.\nOf course, you can control the state of that Dialog.
Transition Scene
Transition scene with close

LHTransitionAnimator is a component that extends LHAnimationClipPlayer for scene transitions.

By integrating it with the base classes Scene and ScreenStack, it allows for complete separation of editing areas between developers and designers, enabling efficient work.

Of course, as a senior engineer, you can extend these components.
LHButton is a feature that inherits from Unity.UI.Button.\nCurrently, it only has mutually exclusive input functionality.

LHTextMeshPro is a Text component with localization and dictionary functionality.\nIt includes an EditorTool, allowing for GUI-based text management.\nBecause it uses the TSV format, there is room to create project-specific tools.
